content:
- Read prior Ralph progress, refreshed `.ralph/current_tasks.md` and `.ralph/current_tasks_done.md`, and read the choose-task rules plus active/done task summaries.
- Confirmed the actual codebase is a Go benchmark service (`go.mod`, `Makefile`, `cmd/pg_gobench`, `internal/app`, `internal/httpserver`, `internal/benchrunner`, `internal/benchmarkrun`, `internal/database`) rather than a copied template.
- Candidate resolution: one unblocked failing high-priority bug task exists (`bug-lock-profile-aborts-on-first-contention-error`); the only other failing task is story-99 manual verification and it is blocked by story-09, which already passes, so it is selectable only after bug-first precedence is satisfied.
- Next should be to write the bug task path into `.ralph/current_task.txt` and stop immediately.

- Re-read refreshed task summaries and confirmed active candidates: one high-priority failing bug plus one failing manual verification task.
- Resolved blocking state from the summaries: story-99 manual verification depends on story-09 UI, which is already done, so it is unblocked; however bug-first precedence still requires choosing the remaining failing bug first.
- Wrote `.ralph/current_task.txt` with `.ralph/tasks/bugs/bug-lock-profile-aborts-on-first-contention-error.md`.
- Next agent should execute that bug task with strict red-green TDD and then validate `make check`, `make test`, and `make lint`.

- Read prior progress, current task pointer, and the required `tdd` plus `improve-code-boundaries` skill docs.
- Confirmed the selected task is `.ralph/tasks/bugs/bug-lock-profile-aborts-on-first-contention-error.md`.
- Reading the task file and nearby planning artifacts next to determine whether this turn is still planning-only or full execution.

- The task file has no execution marker yet, so this turn is plan-first per the Ralph workflow.
- Inspected examples of completed bug tasks and plans to match the expected format: task file carries a `<plan>` path and the plan file ends with `NOW EXECUTE` once the design is settled.
- Reading the lock workload and coordinator/runner code paths now to identify the exact boundary where contention escapes as a terminal error.

- Located the behavior bug in code: `internal/benchrunner/runtime.go` records failed operations and then immediately `finish(err)` on any worker error, which explains why a single contention aborts the whole run.
- Existing tests still encode that wrong contract in `internal/benchrunner/runner_test.go` by expecting `StatusFailed` after a lock contention error.
- Reading the fake SQL driver and runtime tests now so the plan can call for a realistic first red test around the `FOR UPDATE NOWAIT` contention path.

- Read the active bug task, inspected the lock workload and runtime path, and identified the root cause: `internal/benchrunner/runtime.go` treats every workload error as terminal after recording it in stats.
- Confirmed the existing lock contention test is modeling the wrong path by failing `SET LOCAL lock_timeout` and still expecting `StatusFailed`.
- Added the planning artifact `.ralph/tasks/bugs/bug-lock-profile-aborts-on-first-contention-error_plans/2026-04-30-lock-profile-contention-plan.md`, linked it from the bug task, and promoted the plan to `NOW EXECUTE`.
- Next turn should execute the plan with strict red-green TDD: first reproduce real `FOR UPDATE NOWAIT` contention through the fake SQL driver, then make contention non-terminal for the lock workload while preserving terminal failures elsewhere.

- Read progress, current task, task file, execution plan, and required `tdd` plus `improve-code-boundaries` skill docs.
- Confirmed the bug task is already in `NOW EXECUTE`; the plan calls for a narrow private runner boundary: count lock contention as failed operations without terminating the run.
- Next: read the typed-error-boundary smell guidance and existing runner tests, then add one RED test that reproduces real `FOR UPDATE NOWAIT` contention before changing implementation.

- Replaced the stale fail-fast lock-contention test with a real RED test in `internal/benchrunner/runner_test.go` that injects an error on the `FOR UPDATE NOWAIT` query path via custom `driver.Rows`.
- Confirmed RED: `go test ./internal/benchrunner -run TestCoordinatorCountsAndSurfacesLockContentionFailures -count=1` fails because the run still transitions to `StatusFailed` immediately after the first contention.
- Next: add a small private non-terminal error boundary inside `internal/benchrunner`, wire the lock workload to use it, and keep all other workload/setup failures terminal.

- Added a private typed error boundary in `internal/benchrunner/operation_error.go`; lock-contention query failures are now wrapped as counted operation errors, and `runtime.go` records them then continues instead of finishing the run.
- Narrowed the lock workload so only the `FOR UPDATE NOWAIT` query path gets non-terminal treatment; setup, pacing, begin/commit, and unrelated workload errors remain terminal.
- The realistic RED test is now green. Next: search for stale fail-fast contention assertions/docs, run broader benchrunner tests, then perform manual Compose verification before the full `make` gates.

- Manual Compose verification partially passed: after 2 seconds the real `/benchmark/results` stayed `running` with non-zero `failed_operations` and lock-contention error text, proving the `FOR UPDATE NOWAIT` path no longer kills the run.
- The bug still reproduces through another observed contention class: by ~7 seconds the run flips to `failed` with `hot update: update account: ERROR: canceling statement due to lock timeout (SQLSTATE 55P03)`.
- Next: add one new RED test for the hot-update lock-timeout path, then extend the same private counted-operation boundary narrowly enough to cover that observed contention failure without making unrelated update errors non-terminal.

- Manual verification exposed a second real failure path, so I added a new RED test for hot-update `UPDATE` failures returning PostgreSQL `SQLSTATE 55P03` lock-timeout errors.
- Implemented a narrow typed classification using `*pgconn.PgError` code `55P03`; `workload_lock.go` now counts hot-update lock-not-available failures as non-terminal while still failing fast for unrelated update errors.
- Both lock-focused coordinator tests should now pass; next is another real Compose run to confirm the full 10-second benchmark ends stopped instead of failing around the hot-update path.

- The real app was still failing because the hot-update lock-timeout could arrive as a plain SQLSTATE-bearing error string rather than a concrete `*pgconn.PgError`.
- Added another RED test for the string-wrapped `SQLSTATE 55P03` path and made it green by extending the private classifier to accept the typed pgconn form first, with a narrow SQLSTATE `55P03` fallback.
- Next: rebuild the Compose app image, rerun the documented manual reproduction end-to-end, and confirm the 10-second lock run now stays alive and ends stopped instead of failing early.
